name: Infosys Limited
executive_summary: Infosys Limited (INFY) screens as a buy on quality, cash generation, and shareholder returns rather than deep book-value cheapness. The quantitative screen passes 10 of 22 models (composite 54%) across cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, and risk families, anchored by a 14.1x P/E, 4.6% yield, 31.4% ROE, and 6.9% FCF yield. The investment case is a scaled Indian IT franchise with resilient margins, rising revenue, and disciplined capital return, trading at a discount to its historical quality premium after sector-wide de-rating on AI-disruption and demand fears. The central debate is whether GenAI becomes a growth accelerant (OpenAI, healthcare, enterprise deals) or compresses legacy services pricing faster than Infosys can pivot.
investment_thesis:
For a value investor, INFY offers a rare combination in global IT: mid-teens earnings power, high ROE, and a cash-backed dividend/buyback programme at a mid-cycle multiple. The screen’s passes on FCF Yield, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, and Financial Health align with a business that converts earnings to cash consistently: Yahoo-sourced FY2026 (year ended March 2026) free cash flow was $3.73bn on net income of $3.31bn, supporting the screen’s 6.9% FCF yield.

Revenue has re-accelerated from +1.9% (FY2024) to +4.6% (FY2026), while operating margins have held near 20% for four years—evidence of pricing discipline and cost control despite wage inflation and utilisation pressure. The 4.6% dividend yield and active buybacks ($2.01bn repurchased in FY2026 per Yahoo cash-flow data) provide a tangible return floor that screens do not fully capture in P/B alone (9.4x reflects asset-light model, not balance-sheet weakness).

Business quality markers include a global delivery footprint, long-dated client relationships (e.g. NHS workforce-management contract reported October 2025), and an AI/services pivot (OpenAI partnership, Sentara healthcare AI collaboration). The screen’s Neff PEGY (0.03) and 11.8% growth input suggest the market is under-pricing earnings durability relative to growth—a classic GARP setup for patient accumulators rather than deep cyclical bets.
financial_review:
Primary filings: One annual 20-F is indexed (filed 15 June 2026, FY ended 31 March 2026). Its body extract is XBRL tag metadata without readable line-item values or narrative risk sections, so quantitative analysis below falls back to `financials_annual.json` (Yahoo, USD). Interim 6-K filings are present in quantity (37 indexed) but available body extracts are cover/index pages only—Q1 FY2026 results for the quarter ended 30 June 2025 are referenced in filings dated 23–28 July 2025 without attached exhibit numbers; later interim results (October 2025–April 2026) are indexed but lack downloadable bodies. This is a material gap for quarter-on-quarter verification.

Annual trends (Yahoo fallback, USD):

| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | FY2026 |
|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Revenue | $18.21bn | $18.56bn | $19.28bn | $20.16bn |
| Net income | $2.98bn | $3.17bn | $3.16bn | $3.31bn |
| Operating margin | 21.0% | 20.7% | 21.1% | 20.3% |
| Net margin | 16.4% | 17.1% | 16.4% | 16.4% |

Revenue growth has improved sequentially; earnings rebounded in FY2026 after a flat FY2025. Margins remain structurally strong for IT services, with gross margin stable around 30%.

Cash flow and capital allocation (Yahoo fallback):

- FY2026: operating cash flow $4.04bn; FCF $3.73bn; dividends paid $2.13bn; share repurchases $2.01bn.
- FY2025: FCF $4.09bn; dividends $2.42bn; no buybacks recorded.
- Cash and equivalents fell to $2.34bn at FY2026 year-end (from $2.86bn), reflecting return of capital; total debt remains modest at ~$0.97bn (primarily lease obligations per balance sheet).

Balance sheet (Yahoo fallback, FY2026): total assets $16.45bn; common equity $9.79bn (down from $11.21bn in FY2025, consistent with buybacks); working capital $5.40bn. Net cash position remains comfortable relative to debt.

Latest quarter (Yahoo quarterly cache, label “2026”): revenue $5.04bn, net income $0.92bn, diluted EPS $0.23. Without interim filing exhibit text, this cannot be cross-checked against reported constant-currency growth or guidance from the earnings release.

Filing-derived qualitative points (20-F XBRL references, no amounts): employee benefit obligations (gratuity, provident fund), tax contingent liabilities (`TaxOtherThanIncomeTaxContingentLiabilityMember`), branch profit tax exposure, and a documented November 2023 cybersecurity incident. Buyback programmes are referenced for February 2023 and December 2025 completions; September 2025 6-K filings confirm board approval of a new tender-route buyback and subsequent postal ballot process.
risks_and_flags:
Cyclical demand: News coverage (July 2026) flags muted Q1 expectations across Indian IT on weak client spending, AI-driven pricing pressure, and geopolitical uncertainty—directly relevant to Infosys’s near-term revenue trajectory.

AI disruption: Sector de-rating after Accenture’s outlook cut (June 2026) and IBM’s preliminary miss (July 2026) highlights market fear that GenAI erodes legacy outsourcing volumes faster than vendors monetise AI services.

Regulatory and tax: 20-F XBRL tags reference tax contingent liabilities and branch profit tax—India/US transfer-pricing and overseas branch taxation remain ongoing overhangs for Indian multinationals.

Cybersecurity and governance: A November 2023 cybersecurity incident is explicitly tagged in the 20-F metadata; no remediation detail is available in the truncated body extract. Operational resilience remains a reputational and client-retention risk.

FX and macro: ~60%+ revenue is USD/Europe-linked while a large cost base is INR-denominated; USD/INR moves directly affect reported margins. Macro context shows a 10-year UST yield of ~4.6% and USD index ~101—relevant for discount-rate and EM-flow sentiment but not used to override the screen signal.

Capital structure: Aggressive FY2026 buybacks ($2.01bn) alongside dividends reduced equity and cash; sustainable if FCF holds, but limits balance-sheet flexibility in a downturn.

Competition: Intense rivalry from TCS, HCLTech, Wipro, and global peers (Accenture, IBM) in AI-led transformation deals; large-deal wins (e.g. NHS) can be lumpy and margin-dilutive in early phases.

Pension/employee costs: Gratuity and provident fund obligations are recurring cost items; wage inflation and attrition in Indian IT remain structural pressures not fully visible in screen metrics.

No going-concern language was found in available filing body extracts.
news_highlights:
Coverage over the past year is moderate—mix of company-specific deal flow and sector-wide sentiment; many headlines are price-commentary or trading-oriented rather than fundamental.

Strategy and AI:
- “Infosys (INFY) Stock Partners With OpenAI to Drive Enterprise AI” (CoinCentral, 22 April 2026)
- “Infosys Limited (INFY) Partners with Sentara To Enable AI Integration and Use in Hospitals” (Yahoo Finance, 8 July 2026)
- “Infosys (INFY) Stock Gains Momentum Following CMMI AI Framework Pilot Achievement” (MoneyCheck, 11 June 2026)
- “Can Intel's AI Push With Infosys Deal Drive Long-Term Growth?” (TradingView, 9 March 2026)

Large contracts and expansion:
- “Infy bags $1.6bn NHS workforce management deal” (The Times of India, 15 October 2025)
- “Infosys Expands Global Footprint with New Subsidiary in Canada” (scanx.trade, 29 July 2025)
- “Infosys expands digital overhaul for 46,000 Nottingham students” (Stock Titan, 24 March 2026)
- “Infosys Expands Metro Bank Partnership for Workday-Powered Financial Transformation” (scanx.trade, 29 October 2025)

Capital return:
- Board consideration of buyback announced (Business Today/top-stocks coverage, 8 September 2025)
- Buyback approved by board (6-K filing body, 11 September 2025)
- Postal ballot and tender-offer buyback process (6-K, 26–29 September 2025)

Sector and sentiment:
- “Indian IT firms face muted Q1 as AI shift, weak demand weigh” (Yahoo Finance, 6 July 2026)
- “Infosys (INFY) Stock Drops 6% on IT Sector Weakness After Accenture Cut Growth Outlook” (FXLeaders, 19 June 2026)
- “Software Stocks Sink as IBM Miss Delivers ‘Devastating Blow’” (Bloomberg/Yahoo Finance, 14 July 2026)
- “Infosys Limited Just Missed Earnings - But Analysts Have Updated Their Models” (simplywall.st, 17 January 2026)

Governance (non-operational): “Nandan Nilekani leaves GP role at Fundamentum as it launches $200M third fund” (Yahoo Finance, 9 July 2026)—co-founder remains anchor investor; not a change in Infosys management.

Analyst interest: Infosys appears on FY2027 top-pick lists (Business Today, 3 April 2026) and IT sector target-price roundups (Business Today, 17 February 2026), suggesting institutional support despite sector weakness.
research_rationale: Deep research broadly confirms the screen’s buy signal on cash-backed quality and shareholder returns, tempered by sector cyclicality, AI-disruption uncertainty, and incomplete interim filing data for recent quarters.
